humanities_role_explanation: Uses literary narrative texts (novels) as the domain for evaluating discourse-level language understanding
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Text-only benchmark using narrative passages from novels where models must predict the final word of a target sentence given broader discourse context
analytical_tasks_explanation: Word prediction requiring broad discourse understanding where the target word cannot be guessed from local sentence context alone but becomes predictable with full passage context. Tasks involve coreference resolution, bridging inferences, and understanding narrative coherence.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: General language modeling benchmark designed to test broad discourse understanding capabilities

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates traditional language models (RNN, LSTM, N-gram, Memory Networks) but not modern generative LLMs like GPT-4

llm_is_primary_system.note: Language models are the primary systems being benchmarked for discourse understanding

arts_humanities_data.note: Literature and Literary Theory - uses narrative passages from unpublished novels from BookCorpus

quantitative_metrics.note: Accuracy (percentage of correctly predicted target words), perplexity, and median rank of correct word

english_language.note: English language paper and benchmark using English novels

summary: LAMBADA introduces a word prediction benchmark using narrative passages where humans can guess the final word with full context but not from the last sentence alone. The dataset consists of 10,022 passages from novels designed to test computational models' ability to understand broad discourse context rather than relying on local linguistic cues.
key_findings: All tested language models (RNN, LSTM, N-gram, Memory Networks) achieved near 0% accuracy on LAMBADA while performing well (15-22% accuracy) on a control dataset, demonstrating that current models fail at discourse-level understanding despite strong local language modeling capabilities.
paper_name: Evaluating Automatic Speech Recognition Systems on Multilingual Audio Benchmarks
